title: MaestroMotif: Skill Design from Artificial Intelligence Feedback

reviews.summary: This paper proposes MaestroMotif, an AI-assisted skill design framework that marries LLM preference-based reward design with LLM-generated programmatic coordination of options, enabling reinforcement learning of low-level skills and zero-shot composition for new tasks specified in natural language. The method: (1) translates language skill descriptions into per-skill reward models via LLM preference elicitation (Motif), (2) uses an LLM to generate code for initiation/termination conditions and a training-time policy-over-skills, (3) trains skill policies with RL, and (4) at deployment, prompts an LLM code generator to instantly synthesize a task-specific skill-selection policy that composes the learned skills. On a comprehensive suite of navigation, interaction, and composite tasks in NetHack, MaestroMotif substantially outperforms zero-shot LLM policies and task-specific RL methods, demonstrating strong zero-shot controllability from language.
strengths: Integrates RL from LLM preference feedback (per-skill reward modeling) with LLM code generation for skill initiation/termination and high-level policy programming, yielding a practical and automated pipeline for option construction and composition.
Uses code as a high-level policy representation, enabling complex logic, memory, and temporal abstraction that would be hard to learn with a neural meta-controller.
Introduces a training-time policy-over-skills, generated from language, to induce an emergent curriculum and align state distributions for effective multi-skill learning.
Augments preference elicitation with state deltas and partial stats to provide a compressed trajectory context, improving specificity of learned rewards per skill.

Comprehensive evaluation on NetHack across three challenging categories (navigation, interaction, composite), with nine seeds and error bars, and comparisons to multiple strong baselines (zero-shot ReAct, RL with AI-feedback rewards, embedding similarity, and a privileged-reward PPO).

Ablations on hierarchical architecture (one-hot conditioned shared policy vs multi-head), synchronous vs asynchronous skill training, and LLM scale for the code generator, each linked to clear performance impacts.
Clear evidence that score-maximizing agents do not solve the proposed goal-centric tasks, highlighting the value of skill-centric, language-shaped objectives.
The overall system design and four-phase pipeline are clearly articulated, with figures illustrating data flows, code-generation prompts, and deployment-time policy synthesis.
Task descriptions, skill definitions, and the reasoning behind skill choices (e.g., bottleneck options) are well motivated and connected to prior HRL ideas.
Demonstrates a practical path for human-AI collaboration in skill design, reducing manual engineering and enabling rich zero-shot control from language in a difficult, long-horizon environment.
Provides a compelling bridge between abstract language-level specifications and sensorimotor control, suggesting a general paradigm applicable beyond NetHack.
weaknesses: Heavy dependence on very large LLMs (e.g., Llama 405B) for reliable code generation raises cost and accessibility concerns; performance appears sensitive to this scale.
The approach presumes an a priori human-curated skill set and training-time interleaving specification; coverage gaps in the skill library may limit expressivity for new tasks.
Executing LLM-generated code inside the agent loop raises safety and reproducibility concerns unless tightly sandboxed; details of sandboxing and failure handling are limited.
Missing a comparison to a learned neural high-level meta-controller trained over the same skill set (e.g., PPO over options) to isolate the benefit of programmatic policies at test time.
Limited analysis of robustness to noisy or inconsistent LLM preference labels and of inter-annotator agreement; no study of sensitivity to prompt variations for reward modeling or code generation.
Compute and data budgets are not fully normalized across methods; training costs (wall-clock, environment frames, LLM calls) for MaestroMotif vs task-specific RL baselines are not reported in detail.

Some implementation details (e.g., code refinement procedure, error handling when generated initiation/termination is incorrect, exact sandboxing) are deferred to appendices; more in-paper clarity would help assess reliability.
Minor equation formatting artifacts and occasional typos do not hinder understanding but slightly distract.

Limited empirical comparisons to classical HRL with learned subgoal discovery (e.g., option-critic variants, skill graphs) and programmatic policy learning in DSLs; more thorough baselines could strengthen claims about the necessity of code-generated controllers.
Connections to language-shaped RL or TAMER-style interactive feedback are cited but not empirically contrasted.
reviews.questions:
1. How is LLM-generated code sandboxed and validated before execution during both training and deployment? Do you collect quantitative statistics on unit-test pass rates and runtime failures, and were any manual fixes required?
2. What are the environment interaction budgets and wall-clock compute for (a) skill reward training, (b) skill policy RL training, and (c) per-task code generation, compared to each task-specific RL baseline? Please include LLM inference costs.
3. How robust are results to prompt variations for (i) reward elicitation and (ii) code generation? Can you report sensitivity analyses and failure cases?
4. Could a learned neural meta-controller over the same options (trained with RL or behavior cloning from the code policy) close the gap to the programmatic policy? If attempted, please share results.
5. How does performance degrade when removing or altering one of the five skills (e.g., replacing with a more generic “explore” option)? Any evidence that the approach generalizes to different skill libraries?
6. Do you observe label noise or inconsistencies in LLM preferences across queries? Did you measure inter-annotator reliability (e.g., multiple LLM runs or temperatures) and its effect on reward quality?
7. Are initiation/termination predicates consistently reliable in diverse states? Have you considered learning these predicates (e.g., from demonstrations or via classifier training) to complement code-generated logic?
8. To what extent does the approach generalize beyond NetHack? What would be needed to port MaestroMotif to 3D embodied environments or robotics (e.g., perception grounding, actuation latencies)?
9. How do you prevent reward hacking or degenerate behaviors with per-skill rewards, especially for interaction-centric skills (Merchant/Worshipper)? Any safeguards or diagnostics?
10. Will you release prompts, generated code, reward models, and training scripts to enhance reproducibility?
reviews.assessment: MaestroMotif is a substantial and timely contribution that advances the practical automation of hierarchical RL through human-AI collaboration. By combining LLM-driven per-skill reward learning with code-generated option structure and high-level policy composition, the authors deliver an agent that can be reprogrammed zero-shot from language to perform complex, long-horizon tasks in NetHack—significantly surpassing strong baselines. The empirical study is broad and supported by informative ablations, and the methodology is well-motivated and sound. The main caveats are dependence on very large LLMs, limited discussion of code-safety and reproducibility details, and a few missing baselines (notably a learned neural meta-controller and stronger HRL comparators). Even with these limitations, the paper’s core ideas and results are compelling and will be of high interest to the ICLR community. I recommend acceptance, with encouragement to expand robustness, safety, and compute reporting, and to add a neural meta-controller baseline if feasible.
